questions.question: What was the significance of the Opium Wars between Britain and China?
options: The Opium Wars were civil wars within China between rival dynasties; Britain provided military advisors to both sides

The First (1839-42) and Second (1856-60) Opium Wars were conflicts in which Britain forced China to open its ports to trade, including the opium trade. China suffered humiliating defeats, ceded Hong Kong to Britain, and was forced to grant extraterritoriality to foreigners. The wars began China's 'Century of Humiliation' that shapes Chinese foreign policy to this day
The Opium Wars were trade disputes resolved peacefully through arbitration; Britain compensated China for lost opium revenues
The Opium Wars were fought between China and France over control of Indochina; Britain remained neutral throughout both conflicts
questions.answer: The First (1839-42) and Second (1856-60) Opium Wars were conflicts in which Britain forced China to open its ports to trade, including the opium trade. China suffered humiliating defeats, ceded Hong Kong to Britain, and was forced to grant extraterritoriality to foreigners. The wars began China's 'Century of Humiliation' that shapes Chinese foreign policy to this day
questions.explanation: Britain controlled the opium trade from India to China. China's Qing government banned opium (mass addiction, silver drain), confiscated British opium stocks in 1839. Britain declared war. Treaty of Nanking (1842): China ceded Hong Kong, opened 5 treaty ports, paid £21 million indemnity. Second Opium War: British-French forces sacked Beijing, burned the Summer Palace. Treaty of Tientsin: more ports, legalization of opium trade, Christian missionaries allowed. The 'unequal treaties' — extraterritoriality, tariff caps, most-favored-nation clauses — undermined Chinese sovereignty until the 1940s. 'Century of Humiliation' (1839-1949) is foundational to contemporary Chinese nationalism.
questions.memory_hook: Britain went to war with China to force China to keep buying opium — a drug Britain sold from its Indian colonies. When China tried to stop a mass addiction crisis by seizing British opium, Britain sent warships. This is not propaganda: it is what the Opium Wars were. The 'Century of Humiliation' China's leaders still invoke — unequal treaties, extraterritoriality, foreign zones in Chinese cities — traces directly to these wars. Understanding this history explains why Chinese leaders frame Western criticism of China as continuation of imperial humiliation.

questions.question: What was the significance of the Congress of Berlin (1884-85)?
options: The Congress of Berlin in 1884-85 was a peace conference ending the Franco-Prussian War and redrawing French borders

The Berlin Conference (1884-85) was a meeting of 14 European powers that established rules for colonizing Africa. European nations claimed African territory by notifying others and establishing 'effective occupation.' Africa was divided with almost no regard for existing African kingdoms, ethnic groups, or communities. The borders drawn at Berlin still define most African nations today
The Congress of Berlin was a trade agreement establishing the first free trade zone in sub-Saharan Africa
The Berlin Conference was called by African leaders to negotiate with European colonial powers; African delegates participated as equal partners in the negotiations
questions.answer: The Berlin Conference (1884-85) was a meeting of 14 European powers that established rules for colonizing Africa. European nations claimed African territory by notifying others and establishing 'effective occupation.' Africa was divided with almost no regard for existing African kingdoms, ethnic groups, or communities. The borders drawn at Berlin still define most African nations today
questions.explanation: Berlin Conference (November 1884 – February 1885): called by Otto von Bismarck, attended by 14 nations (including US), no African representatives. By 1914, European powers controlled 90% of Africa. The Congo Free State was given to King Leopold II of Belgium personally (not Belgium as a state) — who proceeded to kill an estimated 10 million Congolese through forced rubber quotas enforced by severing hands. The conference created straight-line borders cutting across 10,000 years of African political geography — dividing the Yoruba between Nigeria and Benin, the Somali across 5 nations, the Maasai between Kenya and Tanzania — borders that created conflict still ongoing today.
questions.memory_hook: At the Berlin Conference, King Leopold II of Belgium received the Congo — an area 80 times the size of Belgium — as his personal property. He ran it like a plantation, forcing Congolese to harvest rubber under threat of amputation. An estimated 10 million people died. When journalists exposed this, international pressure forced Belgium to take the colony from Leopold. His personal reign over the Congo is considered one of the first documented genocides of the 20th century. His statue still stands in Belgium, increasingly controversial.

questions.question: What was the significance of the Battle of Thermopylae?
options: The Battle of Thermopylae (479 BC) was Persia's final defeat; Athens routed the Persian fleet, ending the Persian Wars

The Battle of Thermopylae (480 BC) was a three-day stand by 300 Spartans (and ~7,000 Greek allies) under King Leonidas against Xerxes's massive Persian army. Though the Greeks were ultimately defeated and killed, their delaying action allowed the Greek fleet to evacuate Athens and enabled the Greek navy to defeat Persia at Salamis, ultimately ending the Persian invasion
The Battle of Thermopylae was a decisive Greek victory; the 300 Spartans killed over 100,000 Persian soldiers in a single day and turned Xerxes back to Persia
The Battle of Thermopylae was a Roman victory over Greek city-states; it established Roman dominance over the Aegean Sea
questions.answer: The Battle of Thermopylae (480 BC) was a three-day stand by 300 Spartans (and ~7,000 Greek allies) under King Leonidas against Xerxes's massive Persian army. Though the Greeks were ultimately defeated and killed, their delaying action allowed the Greek fleet to evacuate Athens and enabled the Greek navy to defeat Persia at Salamis, ultimately ending the Persian invasion
questions.explanation: Thermopylae ('Hot Gates') — a narrow coastal pass where Leonidas positioned 300 Spartans plus ~7,000 Greek allies to hold Xerxes's army (estimated 100,000-300,000). For two days they held; a Greek traitor (Ephialtes) showed Persians a mountain path to flank the position. Leonidas sent most Greeks away; 300 Spartans, 700 Thespians, and 400 Thebans stayed. All died. The delay allowed Greek evacuation of Athens and Themistocles to position the navy at Salamis. The subsequent Battle of Salamis (480 BC) destroyed the Persian fleet. Plataea (479 BC) ended the land invasion. 'Come back with your shield or on it' and 'Molon labe' (Come and take them) are Spartan phrases associated with Thermopylae.
questions.memory_hook: Leonidas sent his young men home — keeping only the 300 Spartans who had living sons, ensuring their family lines would continue. He knew they would all die. They chose to stay. The Thespians (700) also stayed by choice; their city of Thespiae had no similar tradition of battlefield honor. They are rarely mentioned. The 300 Spartans get the memorial; the 700 Thespians who also died voluntarily are largely forgotten. The phrase 'Molon labe' (Come and take them) — Leonidas's response when Xerxes demanded the Greeks surrender their weapons — became a symbol of armed defiance in Western culture.

questions.question: What is the significance of the America's Cup in sailing?
options: The America's Cup is an annual sailing race between the US and UK; it has been held in Newport, Rhode Island every year since 1851

The America's Cup is the oldest international trophy in sport, first awarded in 1851 when the schooner America beat British yachts around the Isle of Wight. The US held the Cup continuously for 132 years (1851-1983) — the longest winning streak in sports history — before Australia II ended it. The Cup features cutting-edge yacht design and attracts billionaire syndicates competing with the most technologically advanced sailing vessels ever built
The America's Cup was first held in 1900; the US has won it three times and Australia holds it currently
The America's Cup is a crewed ocean race across the Atlantic; only professional sailors with 10+ years of ocean experience may compete
questions.answer: The America's Cup is the oldest international trophy in sport, first awarded in 1851 when the schooner America beat British yachts around the Isle of Wight. The US held the Cup continuously for 132 years (1851-1983) — the longest winning streak in sports history — before Australia II ended it. The Cup features cutting-edge yacht design and attracts billionaire syndicates competing with the most technologically advanced sailing vessels ever built
questions.explanation: The America's Cup trophy ('The Auld Mug') was donated after the schooner America won a race around the Isle of Wight in 1851, beating the entire Royal Yacht Squadron's fleet. 132-year US win streak (longest in any international sport) ended when Australia II (with revolutionary winged keel designed by Ben Lexcen) defeated Dennis Conner's Liberty in 1983. Since then: New Zealand has won twice, Switzerland (Alinghi) twice, Oracle Team USA twice. Modern AC boats: foiling catamarans that fly above the water at 50+ knots. The current 37th Cup (2024) was held in Barcelona, won by INEOS Britannia — UK winning for the first time since 1851.
questions.memory_hook: The America's Cup had the longest winning streak in international sports history: 132 years. When Australia II broke it in 1983, the Australian Prime Minister declared a national holiday ('any boss who sacks a worker for not coming in today is a bum'). The winged keel on Australia II caused enormous controversy — the New York Yacht Club tried to disqualify it on technicality. It was allowed. The US had held the Cup since 1851; losing it was treated as a national sports catastrophe.

questions.question: What is the Olympic decathlon and who is considered its greatest modern competitor?
options: The decathlon is a two-day Olympic track and field event comprising 10 disciplines: 100m, long jump, shot put, high jump, 400m (day one) and 110m hurdles, discus, pole vault, javelin, 1500m (day two). Kevin Mayer of France set the world record (9,126 points) in 2018. The decathlon winner is often called the 'world's greatest athlete'
The decathlon is a 15-event test held only at the World Championships; Ashton Eaton of the US holds the world record with 9,045 points
The decathlon is a swimming event combining 10 strokes; it was introduced at the 1996 Atlanta Olympics
The decathlon is a team event where each member runs one discipline; the USA has won all Olympic decathlons since 1976
questions.answer: The decathlon is a two-day Olympic track and field event comprising 10 disciplines: 100m, long jump, shot put, high jump, 400m (day one) and 110m hurdles, discus, pole vault, javelin, 1500m (day two). Kevin Mayer of France set the world record (9,126 points) in 2018. The decathlon winner is often called the 'world's greatest athlete'
questions.explanation: Decathlon scoring: each performance earns points on a formula; totals determine ranking. Ashton Eaton (US) won gold at London 2012 and Rio 2016, setting world records both times. Kevin Mayer (France) broke Eaton's record at 2018 World Championships with 9,126 points. Daley Thompson (UK) won gold at Moscow 1980 and Los Angeles 1984. Jim Thorpe won gold at the 1912 Stockholm Olympics; his medals were later controversially stripped for alleged professionalism and only restored posthumously in 1982. 'World's greatest athlete' title traces to King Gustav V of Sweden telling Thorpe: 'You, sir, are the greatest athlete in the world.'
questions.memory_hook: Jim Thorpe won both the pentathlon and decathlon at the 1912 Olympics — the only person ever to win both at the same Games. He was also an NFL and MLB professional athlete. The IOC stripped his medals in 1913, claiming he had been paid as a semi-professional baseball player. He died in 1953. In 1982 — 29 years after his death — the IOC restored his medals. His family received replicas. The original medals have never been found. He is widely considered one of the greatest athletes in American sports history.

questions.question: Which 1962 novel by Ken Kesey was set in a psychiatric ward and became a counterculture classic?
options: The Bell Jar
Catch-22
Slaughterhouse-Five
One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
questions.answer: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
questions.explanation: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest (1962) by Ken Kesey is narrated by 'Chief' Bromden, a half-Native American patient in an Oregon psychiatric hospital who pretends to be deaf-mute. Randle McMurphy (Jack Nicholson in the 1975 film) is a criminal who fakes insanity to serve his sentence in the hospital rather than prison. He clashes with Nurse Ratched (the controlling, oppressive authority). The novel critiques institutional power, conformity, and the medicalization of nonconformity. It became a counterculture text; Kesey himself was a prominent figure in the 1960s counterculture.
questions.memory_hook: Ken Kesey wrote One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest while working as a night attendant in a psychiatric ward at a VA hospital — and while volunteering for CIA-funded experiments with LSD and other psychedelic drugs. He based the characters on patients he met. The 1975 film with Jack Nicholson won all five major Oscars (Picture, Director, Actor, Actress, Screenplay) — only the second film to do so. Kesey hated the film because McMurphy was the hero rather than Chief Bromden (the narrator). He refused to watch it.

questions.question: What was the significance of Andy Warhol and Pop Art?
options: Andy Warhol was a Soviet artist who created Pop Art as socialist realism depicting consumer goods

Andy Warhol (1928-1987) was the leading figure of Pop Art — a movement that embraced mass-produced commercial imagery (Campbell's Soup Cans, Brillo boxes, celebrity portraits) as fine art. Pop Art challenged distinctions between 'high' art and popular culture, and between original and reproduction. Warhol's Factory was a studio/social scene; his silk-screen prints of Marilyn Monroe, Mao Zedong, and Elvis remain iconic. He said: 'In the future, everyone will be world-famous for 15 minutes'
Andy Warhol was primarily a sculptor; his most famous works are large metal installations at the Museum of Modern Art in New York
Pop Art was invented by Jackson Pollock as a reaction against abstract expressionism; Warhol was a minor follower
questions.answer: Andy Warhol (1928-1987) was the leading figure of Pop Art — a movement that embraced mass-produced commercial imagery (Campbell's Soup Cans, Brillo boxes, celebrity portraits) as fine art. Pop Art challenged distinctions between 'high' art and popular culture, and between original and reproduction. Warhol's Factory was a studio/social scene; his silk-screen prints of Marilyn Monroe, Mao Zedong, and Elvis remain iconic. He said: 'In the future, everyone will be world-famous for 15 minutes'
questions.explanation: Warhol grew up in Pittsburgh, the son of Carpatho-Rusyn immigrants, and was a successful commercial illustrator before becoming a fine artist. Campbell's Soup Cans (1962) — 32 paintings of different varieties — he exhibited arranged like grocery store shelves. He was shot by radical feminist Valerie Solanas in 1968 (survived). The Factory attracted Lou Reed, The Velvet Underground, Edie Sedgwick, Truman Capote. Shot Dead: Jean-Michel Basquiat (who died of heroin, 1988; Warhol had died in 1987). Warhol's quip about '15 minutes of fame' predicts social media celebrity culture with eerie accuracy. His estate is worth ~$228 million.
questions.memory_hook: Andy Warhol exhibited 32 identical-looking Campbell's Soup cans — one for each variety — arranged on shelves. The gallery across the street responded by displaying actual Campbell's Soup cans for sale (33 cents each). This was not entirely missing the point. Warhol claimed he ate Campbell's Soup for lunch every day for 20 years. Whether this was true or performance was always unclear with Warhol, which was rather the point. His later work predicts Instagram influencer culture: celebrity, repetition, serialization, the blurring of commercial and artistic value.

questions.question: Which 1986 film by Oliver Stone depicted the Vietnam War from the perspective of an infantry soldier and won Best Picture?
options: Full Metal Jacket
Born on the Fourth of July
Apocalypse Now
Platoon
questions.explanation: Platoon (1986) by Oliver Stone was the first major Hollywood film about Vietnam directed by someone who actually served there. Stone drew on his own combat experience with the 25th Infantry Division. The film depicts Private Chris Taylor (Charlie Sheen) torn between two sergeants — the disciplined, moral Elias (Willem Dafoe) and the brutal, nihilistic Barnes (Tom Berenger). It won Best Picture and Best Director at the 1987 Oscars. Platoon was the first of Stone's Vietnam trilogy, followed by Born on the Fourth of July (1989) and Heaven & Earth (1993).
questions.memory_hook: Oliver Stone served as an infantryman in Vietnam from 1967-68, was wounded twice, and received the Bronze Star. He wrote Platoon immediately after returning. Hollywood wouldn't make it for 10 years. The film's moral center is the tension between Elias (the good man who dies) and Barnes (the survivalist who becomes a killer). The Elias death scene — arms outstretched like a crucifixion, helicopters above — is one of the most iconic images in Vietnam War cinema. Stone said he was trying to exorcise his own experience; the film grossed $138 million on a $6 million budget.

questions.question: What are the Great Plains of North America and why are they agriculturally important?
options: The Great Plains are a forested plateau in the eastern United States; they were cleared for farming in the 19th century

The Great Plains are a vast grassland region stretching from Canada south through the central US to Texas, east of the Rocky Mountains. They overlie the Ogallala Aquifer, one of the world's largest groundwater systems. The region produces the majority of US wheat, corn, and cattle. The 1930s Dust Bowl — caused by drought and over-plowing — turned much of it to desert temporarily, displacing 3.5 million people
The Great Plains are located west of the Cascade Range in Oregon and Washington; they form the primary agricultural region of the Pacific Northwest
The Great Plains are an elevated plateau in the American Southwest; they receive abundant rainfall from the Gulf of Mexico and require no irrigation
questions.answer: The Great Plains are a vast grassland region stretching from Canada south through the central US to Texas, east of the Rocky Mountains. They overlie the Ogallala Aquifer, one of the world's largest groundwater systems. The region produces the majority of US wheat, corn, and cattle. The 1930s Dust Bowl — caused by drought and over-plowing — turned much of it to desert temporarily, displacing 3.5 million people
questions.explanation: The Great Plains: roughly 1.1 million sq miles from Alberta/Saskatchewan south to Texas. Natural: tall-grass prairie (east), mixed-grass, short-grass (west). The Ogallala Aquifer underlies 174,000 sq miles and took 6-30 million years to accumulate; current extraction rates draw it down faster than it replenishes (90% of usage is irrigation). At current rates, significant depletion within 50-100 years. Dust Bowl (1930-36): 100 million acres stripped of topsoil; 3.5 million fled to California (Steinbeck's Grapes of Wrath). Causes: drought + monoculture farming destroying native grass root systems + mechanization enabling mass cultivation without conservation.
questions.memory_hook: The Ogallala Aquifer is being pumped faster than it refills — and it took millions of years to fill. Farmers are extracting what took geological time to accumulate, at a rate that could exhaust significant portions within a human lifetime. When it goes, a large fraction of US food production goes with it. This is not a distant hypothetical: parts of Kansas and Texas are already experiencing severe depletion. The Dust Bowl of the 1930s — the last catastrophic failure of Great Plains agriculture — displaced 3.5 million people. The aquifer crisis is happening slowly enough that it rarely makes headlines.

questions.question: What is the significance of the Strait of Hormuz to global energy markets?
options: The Strait of Hormuz connects the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean; it carries approximately 10% of global shipping

The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and Arabian Sea. Approximately 20-21 million barrels of oil per day pass through it — roughly 20% of global oil consumption and ~30% of seaborne oil trade. Any closure would immediately affect global oil prices. Iran periodically threatens to close it; the US Navy's Fifth Fleet is based in Bahrain partly to keep it open
The Strait of Hormuz was the site of the decisive 1991 Gulf War naval battle; the US destroyed Iraq's navy there
The Strait of Hormuz is controlled by Saudi Arabia and the UAE jointly; both nations charge tolls on tankers transiting the waterway
questions.answer: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman connecting the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and Arabian Sea. Approximately 20-21 million barrels of oil per day pass through it — roughly 20% of global oil consumption and ~30% of seaborne oil trade. Any closure would immediately affect global oil prices. Iran periodically threatens to close it; the US Navy's Fifth Fleet is based in Bahrain partly to keep it open
questions.explanation: Strait of Hormuz: 21 miles wide at narrowest point; two-lane shipping corridor (2 miles each direction). Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Iraq, and Iran all export oil through it. Alternative routes: Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline to the Red Sea (capacity ~2.4 mbpd vs. 20 mbpd through Hormuz) — completely inadequate substitute. Iran's Revolutionary Guard has seized tankers and planted mines in the Strait. The 1987-88 'Tanker War' (Iran-Iraq War) involved both sides attacking shipping; the US escorted Kuwaiti tankers. Each Iranian threat to close Hormuz sends oil prices spiking globally.
questions.memory_hook: The Strait of Hormuz is 21 miles wide at its narrowest, with a functional shipping corridor of just a few miles. Through this bottleneck flows roughly 20% of the world's oil. There is no adequate alternative route. This makes it the most strategically critical waterway in the world for global energy prices. When Iran's Revolutionary Guard seizes a tanker or mines the strait, oil prices spike worldwide within hours. The US Fifth Fleet exists in Bahrain largely to prevent that from happening on a sustained basis.

questions.question: What is the theory of plate tectonics and what evidence supports it?
options: Plate tectonics holds that Earth's continents drift on a molten interior; the theory was proposed in 1960 and has been rejected by most geologists since 2000

Plate tectonics describes Earth's outer layer (lithosphere) as divided into ~15 major rigid plates moving on the asthenosphere. Continents drift (Pangaea began breaking up ~200 million years ago), oceanic crust is created at mid-ocean ridges and destroyed at subduction zones, and earthquakes/volcanoes occur at plate boundaries. Evidence: matching coastlines (Americas/Africa), identical fossils across ocean basins, seafloor spreading (magnetic striping), GPS measuring plate movement (centimeters per year)
Plate tectonics was disproven in the 1970s; modern geology holds that continents are stationary and earthquakes result from internal Earth vibrations
Plate tectonics only explains ocean-floor geology; continental geology is governed by a separate theory called 'crustal rebound'
questions.answer: Plate tectonics describes Earth's outer layer (lithosphere) as divided into ~15 major rigid plates moving on the asthenosphere. Continents drift (Pangaea began breaking up ~200 million years ago), oceanic crust is created at mid-ocean ridges and destroyed at subduction zones, and earthquakes/volcanoes occur at plate boundaries. Evidence: matching coastlines (Americas/Africa), identical fossils across ocean basins, seafloor spreading (magnetic striping), GPS measuring plate movement (centimeters per year)
questions.explanation: Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift (1912) based on coastline matching and fossil evidence; he was ridiculed for 50 years because he couldn't explain the mechanism. Seafloor spreading (Harry Hess, 1962) — new crust created at mid-ocean ridges, older crust subducted — provided the mechanism. Magnetic striping: Earth's magnetic field reverses periodically; oceanic crust records each reversal as a stripe on either side of ridges, proving crust moves away from ridges symmetrically. GPS now measures plate speeds: Pacific Plate ~7 cm/year, Atlantic widening ~2.5 cm/year. Prediction: the Atlantic will eventually close; Africa will collide with Europe.
questions.memory_hook: Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912, pointing out that Africa and South America fit together like puzzle pieces, with identical fossils on both sides of the Atlantic. Geologists rejected him for 50 years because he couldn't explain what moved the continents. The mechanism (seafloor spreading) wasn't discovered until the 1960s. Wegener died on a Greenland ice expedition in 1930, never seeing his theory vindicated. This is one of the most famous examples in science of a correct theory being rejected because the proposer lacked the mechanism — not the evidence.

questions.question: What is the difference between osmosis, diffusion, and active transport in cells?
options: Osmosis and diffusion are the same process; active transport is a form of osmosis requiring sunlight

Diffusion is the passive movement of molecules from high to low concentration. Osmosis is specifically water moving across a semipermeable membrane from low to high solute concentration (equalizing concentrations). Active transport moves molecules against their concentration gradient and requires ATP energy. Cells use all three: diffusion (gases, lipids), osmosis (water balance), active transport (ions like Na+ and K+ via sodium-potassium pump)
Osmosis requires ATP; diffusion requires no energy; active transport requires light
All three processes only occur in plant cells; animal cells lack the membrane structures needed for diffusion
questions.answer: Diffusion is the passive movement of molecules from high to low concentration. Osmosis is specifically water moving across a semipermeable membrane from low to high solute concentration (equalizing concentrations). Active transport moves molecules against their concentration gradient and requires ATP energy. Cells use all three: diffusion (gases, lipids), osmosis (water balance), active transport (ions like Na+ and K+ via sodium-potassium pump)
questions.explanation: Diffusion: any molecule moving passively down concentration gradient — oxygen into cells, CO2 out, perfume spreading through air. Osmosis: specifically water, across semipermeable membrane — red blood cells in hypertonic solution shrivel (crenation); in hypotonic solution burst (lysis). Active transport: Na+/K+ pump moves 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP — maintaining the electrochemical gradient needed for nerve impulses and muscle contraction. Without the Na+/K+ pump, neurons couldn't fire. This is why local anesthetics (blocking Na+ channels) prevent pain signals. It's also why cardiac glycosides (digoxin, from foxglove) affect heart rate: they inhibit the pump in heart muscle.
questions.memory_hook: Local anesthetics like lidocaine work by blocking sodium ion channels — stopping active transport cold in nerve cells near the injection site. Without active transport, neurons can't fire action potentials. You literally cannot transmit pain signals from that area. The sodium-potassium pump (active transport) runs continuously in every neuron in your body right now, consuming ~20-25% of your brain's total ATP. Stopping it — as happens in certain poisonings — causes rapid loss of consciousness. Understanding osmosis and active transport is understanding why you die without enough water, salt, or potassium.
